blocks.content: About two weeks after the hospital, she woke at three in the morning and found him awake.

She had known, somewhere in her sleep, that he hadn't been sleeping. She had been waking partially for nights now and finding the bed warm but wrong — the quality of his stillness different from the stillness of sleep.
She turned over and looked at him in the dark.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: How long have you been awake tonight?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Sorry. I didn't mean to wake you.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You didn't. How long?
He didn't answer right away.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: A while.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: How many nights, Ethan?
A longer pause.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Most of them.
She closed her eyes. Took that in.
She had known. She had let him have it. But two weeks was enough.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Come here.
He moved closer. She put her arm around him — the other way than usual, her holding him, his head against her shoulder.
He let her.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Talk to me.
A long silence. The house dark and quiet around them. December cold against the windows.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I keep thinking about the six minutes.
She held tighter.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I keep going back to it. What I would have done if — what it would have been like if they couldn't—
He stopped.
She felt the breath move through him. Felt him trying to finish a sentence he had no words for.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You don't have to say it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I know. But it's there. Every night, it's just — there.
A long quiet.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I know you're scared.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I'm not scared right now. Right now I'm just awake.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: What's the difference?
He thought about it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Right now I can feel you breathing.
Silence.
She pressed her lips to the top of his head.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I'm still here.
She said it like a promise. Like the truest thing she knew.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I'm still here, Ethan. I'm right here.
He held on.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Whatever time we have — we're in it right now. This is not the before. This is it. We're already in it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yeah.
Barely a word. But enough.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: So let tonight be enough. Just tonight. Let it be enough.
She held him until his breathing slowed.
She held him until he slept.
She was the one who was sick and she was the one holding him and neither of them found anything strange about that.
She lay awake for a while after that — not from fear, but from the particular tenderness of being the one who held. She had been watched over for so long. Carried. Worried about.
Tonight she was the one keeping watch.
And it was the most herself she had felt since coming home.
